serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Huong Snail with Cheese Sauce is approximately 200g, representing a standard restaurant portion of snails in cheese sauce served as a shared dish or individual starter.

nutrient_source.title: Huong Snail With Cheese Sauce (Ốc Hương Sốt Phô Mai) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Huong Snail With Cheese Sauce (cr_id G00011032). All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 9.5g/100g based on the known composition of processed cheese sauce and butter as primary fat contributors; this specific sub-fraction is not separately reported in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Huong Snail with Cheese Sauce. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood and cheese dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 8.85g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from cheese sauce sugars. High protein (16.09g) and fat (23.06g) content significantly blunts glycemic response. GL per 200g serving = (28 × 8.85 × 2) / 100 = 4.96, rounded to 4.9. This is consistent with low-carbohydrate mixed protein-fat dishes in the Sydney GI database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279mg/100g — critically exceeds the 600mg/100g upper limit for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension must strictly avoid this dish; sodium at 1279mg/100g is more than double the 600mg/100g safety threshold, and a single 200g serving delivers approximately 2558mg sodium, exceeding the entire daily recommended limit of 2000mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.85g/100g) and high protein moderate glycemic response, but high saturated fat may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (8.85g/100g) and high protein (16.09g/100g) produce a low glycemic load (GL ~4.9 per serving), making this dish acceptable for blood glucose control in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat (~9.5g/100g) from processed cheese sauce should be limited to avoid worsening insulin resistance, and portion size should be restricted to 100–150g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined high sodium (1279mg/100g) and high saturated fat (~9.5g/100g) present dual cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular risk due to critically elevated sodium (1279mg/100g) contributing to arterial hypertension and estimated saturated fat of approximately 9.5g/100g promoting LDL cholesterol elevation; individuals with cardiovascular disease or at elevated CVD risk should avoid regular consumption and seek lower-sodium, lower-saturated-fat alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5g/100g from processed cheese sauce exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content (~9.5g/100g) from processed cheese sauce and butter is pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate rheumatoid arthritis symptoms by promoting TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity; zero dietary fibre provides no compensatory gut microbiome benefit, and this dish should be avoided or consumed only occasionally in very small portions by RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Huong Snail with Cheese Sauce (Ốc Hương Sốt Phô Mai) is a Vietnamese-origin seafood dish popular in Singapore, featuring Huong snails (Babylonia areolata), a prized gastropod mollusc, cooked in a rich, creamy processed cheese sauce with butter and garlic. The dish is characterised by a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a creamy, indulgent texture from the melted cheese sauce coating the tender snail meat. It is typically served as a shared starter or main dish in Vietnamese seafood restaurants and street-food establishments across Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (16.09g/100g) from the snail meat, high in fat (23.06g/100g) predominantly from the cheese sauce and butter, and low in carbohydrates (8.85g/100g). Calcium content is exceptionally high at 1375mg/100g, largely attributable to the processed cheese component. Sodium is critically elevated at 1279mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated saturated fat of approximately 9.5g/100g from the cheese sauce and butter raises concerns for cardiovascular health and rheumatoid arthritis management. Despite its low glycemic load, the dish should be consumed in moderation due to its high sodium and saturated fat burden. The Vietnamese name Ốc Hương Sốt Phô Mai directly translates as 'fragrant snail with cheese sauce', reflecting the aromatic garlic-butter-cheese preparation style common in Vietnamese seafood cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011032, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from known composition of processed cheese sauce and butter as the HPB record does not separately report this sub-fraction. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate, high-protein seafood and cheese dishes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5g/100g) is estimated based on the known fatty acid composition of processed cheese sauce and butter as the primary fat contributors; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This estimate is consistent with the health_suitability data in the input record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese snail with cheese sauce preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source record (0.0g/100g); this is consistent with a dish composed primarily of mollusc meat and processed cheese sauce with no plant-based fibre sources. 4. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is not certified halal due to the presence of molluscs (snails), which are not universally accepted under halal dietary standards. 6. Allergen advisory: contains dairy (cheese, butter) and molluscs (snail); may contain traces of gluten from processed cheese sauce thickeners.
